answer.rationale: Pan-canonical lexical demonstration. Hebrew אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה ehyeh asher ehyeh (Exod 3:14, the verb הָיָה hayah H1961 in the qal imperfect 1cs, self-naming as Being itself) is the foundational divine self-disclosure, and the Tetragrammaton יהוה YHWH H3068 is its derived third-person form. Greek ἐγώ εἰμι egō eimi (John 8:58, present indicative active 1s with absolute force preceded by πρὶν Ἀβραὰμ γενέσθαι prin Abraam genesthai, 'before Abraham came to be') applies that same self-existence to Jesus. Acts 17:24-25 makes the doctrine explicit: God is not served by human hands ὡς προσδεόμενός τινος hōs prosdeomenos tinos ('as though he needed anything', prosdeomai G4326, the verb of additional need negated of God). Romans 11:35-36 universalizes it: ἐξ αὐτοῦ καὶ δι' αὐτοῦ καὶ εἰς αὐτὸν τὰ πάντα ex autou kai di' autou kai eis auton ta panta ('from him and through him and to him are all things'). Psalm 50:9-12 closes the loop on the Old Testament side: God does not need food because every beast of the forest is already his. John 5:26 attributes the same self-existence to the Son: 'the Father has life in himself, and he has granted the Son also to have life in himself' (ζωὴν ἔχειν ἐν ἑαυτῷ zōēn echein en heautō).
answer.notes: A God who depends on anything outside himself is not the God of Exod 3:14 and Acts 17:25. The verdict rests on pan-canonical lexical demonstration: ehyeh asher ehyeh inaugurates the divine name at the burning bush; Isa 44:6 ('I am the first, and I am the last; besides me there is no god') and Isa 45:5-7 carry the same self-existent monotheism through the prophets; Acts 17:24-25 and Rom 11:35-36 carry it through the apostolic preaching; John 8:58 applies it to Christ. Cult-marker-if-denied is defensible because aseity is what makes biblical monotheism biblical: a derived or dependent God is by definition not the Creator but a creature of something prior. Public-record carriers of denial: Mormonism teaches God is an exalted man (Lorenzo Snow: 'As man now is, God once was; as God now is, man may be'), which is a clean denial of aseity and is treated as outside historic Christianity. Process theism (Whitehead, Hartshorne, John Cobb) holds God and world in mutual becoming, so God's being is genuinely augmented by creation, which denies aseity and falls outside historic Christianity. Open theism (Clark Pinnock, Greg Boyd) typically still affirms aseity formally while qualifying foreknowledge, so it is an in-house evangelical dispute and not cult-marker territory. Strict pantheism / panentheism collapses God into the world. Fruit-vs-precision nuance: most believers cannot articulate aseity by name, but they confess it functionally every time they pray 'Our Father who art in heaven' rather than imagining God as a being who needs them. The cult-marker line falls sharply: full denial of aseity (Mormonism, process theism, pantheism) is outside Christianity; reduced versions that retain self-existence while disputing other attributes are in-house disputes.

stem_audit.notes: The statement 'God is self-existent, deriving His being from nothing outside Himself' is a direct gloss of ehyeh asher ehyeh (Exod 3:14) and Acts 17:25. It asserts the verdict, but every divine-attribute proposition does, and the assertion matches the lexical evidence exactly. No confessional vocabulary is smuggled in (terms like 'simplicity', 'impassibility', or 'pure act' would have indicated Reformed-scholastic loading; they are absent).

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: God is self-existent. He exists from himself and depends on nothing outside himself for his being. The strongest reason from Scripture is the way God names himself at the burning bush in Exodus 3:14. When Moses asks for God's name, God answers 'I AM WHO I AM,' a name built on the verb 'to be.' God's name is, in effect, 'Being itself.' Acts 17:24-25 makes this explicit: 'The God who made the world and everything in it... is not served by human hands, as though he needed anything, since he himself gives to all mankind life and breath and everything.' Psalm 50:9-12 puts the point bluntly. God says, 'If I were hungry, I would not tell you, for the world and its fullness are mine.' Romans 11:35-36 closes the loop: 'Who has given a gift to him that he might be repaid? For from him and through him and to him are all things.' John applies the same self-existence to Jesus at John 8:58: 'Before Abraham was, I am.' The strongest counterargument inside the Bible is the set of passages where God seems to act in human-like ways that suggest dependence or need. Genesis 8:21 says God smelled the pleasing aroma of Noah's sacrifice, as if he were nourished by it. Genesis 2:2-3 says God rested on the seventh day, as if he were tired. Exodus 33:11 says the LORD spoke to Moses face to face, but Exodus 33:20 says no one can see God's face and live. Critics ask, if God is self-existent and needs nothing, why does the Bible picture him smelling sacrifices and resting. The verdict handles this tension by reading these texts the way the Bible itself reads them. Psalm 50:9-12 directly addresses the sacrifice question and says God does not actually eat the food of sacrifices; the aroma language is the language of God accepting worship, not God being fed. Isaiah 40:28 says explicitly that the LORD 'does not faint or grow weary,' which means the seventh-day rest in Genesis 2 is a pattern God establishes for creation, not a recovery from exhaustion. The Bible regularly speaks of God in human terms so that humans can understand him, the way a parent simplifies for a child. These passages describe how God relates to his creatures, not what God needs from them. The doctrine stands on the strongest possible footing in the canon.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Every major historic Christian lineage confesses that God is self-existent and depends on nothing outside himself. Aseity is so foundational that it is rarely listed as its own separate confessional locus; it is folded into the opening line of nearly every creed and confession under the phrase 'one living and true God' or 'eternal, almighty, and incomprehensible God.' The early fathers, including Athanasius and Augustine, taught that God alone has being from himself, while every creature has being only as God grants it. Thomas Aquinas in the Summa treats aseity, simplicity, and eternity as a cluster of necessary divine attributes flowing from God's identification with Being itself at Exodus 3:14. The Catholic Catechism teaches that God is 'He Who Is, without origin and without end.' Lutherans in the Augsburg Confession Article I confess God as eternal and of infinite power. Anglicans in the 39 Articles use the same language. The Reformed confessions, including Westminster 2.2 and Belgic 1, explicitly call God 'self-existent' or describe him as 'of himself.' Methodists in the Articles of Religion repeat the same opening formula. Anabaptists at Dordrecht confess the one eternal almighty God. Pentecostals in the Assemblies of God statement call God 'the eternally self-existent I AM,' echoing Exodus 3:14 directly. Eastern Orthodoxy teaches God as the uncreated and self-existent source of all being. Public-record carriers of denial fall outside historic Christianity. Mormonism teaches that God was once a man who progressed to godhood (the Lorenzo Snow couplet 'As man now is, God once was; as God now is, man may be'), which is a clean denial of aseity. Process theology, associated with Alfred North Whitehead, Charles Hartshorne, and John Cobb, holds that God and the world are in mutual becoming, so God's being is enriched by creation; this is also a denial of aseity. Pantheism and strict panentheism collapse God into the world or make the world a necessary part of God. Open theism, by contrast, formally affirms aseity while qualifying God's foreknowledge of free creaturely choices, so it is treated as an in-house evangelical dispute about a different attribute, not as a denial of aseity. On the bare proposition that God is self-existent, historic Christianity speaks with one voice.

scripture.key_term: אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה; ehyeh asher ehyeh; hayah H1961 qal imperfect 1cs
scripture.force: hayah = to be, become, exist. Qal imperfect 1cs, doubled with the relative pronoun asher. 'I AM WHO I AM' or 'I will be what I will be'. Divine self-naming at the burning bush, ground of the Tetragrammaton YHWH H3068. God's name is built on the verb 'to be', making self-existence the very content of the name

scripture.key_term: πρὶν Ἀβραὰμ γενέσθαι ἐγὼ εἰμί; egō eimi G1473 plus G1510 present indicative active 1s; ginomai G1096 aorist middle infinitive
scripture.force: Jesus contrasts the aorist ginomai ('came to be', a creaturely mode) of Abraham with the present absolute eimi ('I am', a divine mode of self-existence). Direct echo of Exod 3:14 LXX ego eimi ho on. The audience's response (taking up stones to kill him for blasphemy in v.59) shows they understood the claim as appropriation of the divine name

scripture.key_term: οὐδὲ ὑπὸ χειρῶν ἀνθρωπίνων θεραπεύεται προσδεόμενός τινος; prosdeomai G4326 present middle participle; therapeuō G2323
scripture.force: prosdeomai = to need in addition, have further need. Negated with the present middle participle: God is not served 'as one in need of anything'. Paul on the Areopagus directly states divine aseity in lexically explicit form to a pagan audience. The verb is otherwise rare in the New Testament, used here precisely because the proposition needed to be stated technically

scripture.key_term: ὁ θεὸς ὁ ποιήσας τὸν κόσμον; poieō G4160 aorist active participle; kosmos G2889; kyrios G2962
scripture.force: 'The God who made the world and everything in it, being Lord of heaven and earth, does not dwell in temples made by man.' Sets the frame for v.25. The Creator-creature distinction makes aseity necessary: the one who made everything cannot depend on anything within everything

scripture.key_term: ἐξ αὐτοῦ καὶ δι' αὐτοῦ καὶ εἰς αὐτὸν τὰ πάντα; ek G1537 plus dia G1223 plus eis G1519 plus autos G846 plus pas G3956
scripture.force: Three prepositional clauses ascribing the totality of being to God as source, instrument, and goal. 'From him and through him and to him are all things'. Paul's doxology after the long argument of Romans 9 to 11. If everything is from him and to him, nothing is prior to him or external to him on which he might depend

scripture.key_term: τίς προέδωκεν αὐτῷ καὶ ἀνταποδοθήσεται αὐτῷ; prodidōmi G4272 aorist active indicative 3s; antapodidōmi G467 future passive 3s
scripture.force: prodidōmi = to give first, give beforehand. 'Who has first given to him, that it might be repaid to him?' Quoting Job 41:11 LXX. Rhetorical question with the obvious answer 'no one'. Directly denies that God is in debt to any creature for anything, the precise content of aseity

scripture.key_term: כִּי־לִי כָל־חַיְתוֹ־יָעַר בְּהֵמוֹת בְּהַרְרֵי־אָלֶף; kol H3605; chay H2416; behemah H929
scripture.force: 'For every beast of the forest is mine, the cattle on a thousand hills'. God's first-person speech rejecting the idea that he eats the sacrifices. The lemma kol H3605 establishes universal divine ownership: God needs nothing from the worshipper because everything already belongs to him

scripture.key_term: אִם־אֶרְעַב לֹא־אֹמַר לָךְ כִּי־לִי תֵבֵל וּמְלֹאָהּ; raab H7456; tebel H8398; melo H4393
scripture.force: raab = to be hungry. 'If I were hungry, I would not tell you, for the world and its fullness are mine.' Divine self-speech: even the hypothetical of divine need is denied. The Psalter's clearest single statement of aseity, set in deliberate contrast to pagan religion where the gods needed feeding

scripture.key_term: ὥσπερ γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ ἔχει ζωὴν ἐν ἑαυτῷ; zōē G2222; echō G2192 present active indicative 3s; en heautō
scripture.force: 'For as the Father has life in himself, so he has granted the Son also to have life in himself.' The phrase zōēn echein en heautō ('to have life in himself') is the technical New Testament expression of aseity. Attributed first to the Father intrinsically, then granted to the Son, grounding the eternal generation of the Son's self-existent life from the self-existent Father

scripture.key_term: אֲנִי רִאשׁוֹן וַאֲנִי אַחֲרוֹן וּמִבַּלְעָדַי אֵין אֱלֹהִים; rishon H7223; acharon H314; baladay H1107
scripture.force: 'I am the first and I am the last; besides me there is no god.' The exclusive self-existence of YHWH. If God is the first, nothing is prior to him to which he could be indebted for his being. Concordance reinforcement of the ehyeh-Tetragrammaton lemma cluster in the prophets

scripture.key_term: אֱלֹהֵי עוֹלָם יְהוָה לֹא יִיעַף וְלֹא יִיגָע; yaaph H3286; yaga H3021
scripture.force: yaaph = to faint, be weary. yaga = to be exhausted by labor. Both negated of YHWH. 'The everlasting God, the LORD, the Creator of the ends of the earth, does not faint or grow weary.' Direct canonical answer to any anthropomorphic 'God rested' reading that would imply divine exhaustion or need

scripture.key_term: ὁ μόνος ἔχων ἀθανασίαν; monos G3441; athanasia G110
scripture.force: athanasia = deathlessness, immortality (privative alpha plus thanatos). 'Who alone has immortality, who dwells in unapproachable light, whom no one has ever seen or can see.' The adjective monos ('alone') restricts intrinsic deathlessness to God; all creaturely immortality is derived

scripture.key_term: ὁ ὢν καὶ ὁ ἦν καὶ ὁ ἐρχόμενος ὁ παντοκράτωρ; ho ōn; pantokrator G3841
scripture.force: ho ōn ('the one who is') is the LXX rendering of Exod 3:14 ehyeh. Used here of God in the present tense flanked by past and future, marking eternal self-existence. The same phrase is applied to Christ at Rev 22:13. Concordance reinforcement of the Exod 3:14 self-naming in apocalyptic genre

scripture.key_term: וַיָּרַח יְהוָה אֶת־רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ; ruach H7306 hiphil consecutive imperfect; reach H7381; nichoach H5207
scripture.force: ruach hiphil = to smell, perceive odor. 'The LORD smelled the pleasing aroma'. Read literally, suggests God is nourished or pleased by the physical sacrifice in the way a hungry person is by food, which would compromise aseity. Read with Psalm 50:9-12 (God explicitly says he does not eat sacrifices because the world is already his), the smelling language is the language of accepting worship, not of being fed. Anthropomorphic complicating passage required for the verdict

scripture.key_term: וַיִּשְׁבֹּת בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי; shabath H7673 qal consecutive imperfect
scripture.force: shabath = to cease, rest, desist. 'And on the seventh day God finished his work that he had done, and he rested on the seventh day from all his work that he had done.' Read literally, suggests God needed recovery, which would compromise aseity. Read with Isa 40:28 (God 'does not faint or grow weary'), shabath is the language of completion and pattern-setting, not of recovery from exhaustion. Anthropomorphic complicating passage

scripture.key_term: עֹלָה אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחוֹחַ לַיהוָה; olah H5930; ishsheh H801; reach H7381 nichoach H5207
scripture.force: 'A burnt offering, an offering by fire, with a pleasing aroma to the LORD.' Recurring formula throughout Leviticus. Concordance reinforcement of the Gen 8:21 reach nichoach pattern: read literally, sacrifices are food for God, which Psalm 50:9-12 explicitly denies. The Leviticus language is liturgical-accommodative

scripture.key_term: לֹא־אֶקַּח מִבֵּיתְךָ פָר; laqach H3947 qal imperfect 1cs
scripture.force: laqach = to take, receive. Negated: 'I will not accept a bull from your house'. God's own first-person commentary on the sacrificial system, denying that he receives nourishment from any offering. The canonical interpretive key for Gen 8:21 and Lev 1:9: the 'pleasing aroma' language is not divine feeding

scripture.key_term: ἐν αὐτῷ γὰρ ζῶμεν καὶ κινούμεθα καὶ ἐσμέν; zaō G2198; kineō G2795; eimi G1510
scripture.force: 'In him we live and move and have our being.' Three verbs of dependent creaturely existence in the present tense, located 'in him'. The creature's existence is referred to God as its containing ground. Reverses the dependency that pagan religion presupposes: not God in the temple, but the worshipper in God

scripture.key_term: αὐτὸς ἐστιν πρὸ πάντων καὶ τὰ πάντα ἐν αὐτῷ συνέστηκεν; pro G4253; synistēmi G4921 perfect active indicative 3s
scripture.force: synistēmi perfect = to hold together, cohere, consist. 'He is before all things, and in him all things hold together.' Applied to Christ. If created reality coheres in Christ rather than the reverse, then Christ shares the Father's aseity. Concordance reinforcement of John 5:26 and 8:58

competing_lens_verdicts.notes: Athanasius, Augustine, Boethius, Aquinas (Summa I Q.3 on simplicity-aseity-eternity), and the historic Reformers read Exod 3:14 as the divine self-identification with Being itself, from which aseity follows necessarily. The 'God smelled' and 'God rested' texts are read as accommodation, with Ps 50:9-12 and Isa 40:28 as the canonical interpreters. This is the universal historic Christian reading

Open theists (Clark Pinnock, Greg Boyd, John Sanders) formally affirm divine aseity; their distinctive claims concern foreknowledge of free-creature choices and divine responsiveness, not the question of whether God depends on the world for his being. Open theism is in-house evangelical territory on this proposition

Process theology (Alfred North Whitehead, Charles Hartshorne, John Cobb) holds God and world in mutual becoming, so God's actual being is genuinely enriched by creation. This is a clean denial of aseity. Process theists are public-record carriers of the denial and historic Christianity treats them as outside the church on this point

hermeneutics.notes: Verdict rests on a pan-canonical lemma network anchored in hayah H1961 (Exod 3:14 ehyeh asher ehyeh) and its Greek echo eimi G1510 (John 8:58 egō eimi, Rev 1:8 ho ōn). The prosdeomai G4326 of Acts 17:25 is the technical lexical statement that God 'needs nothing in addition', and the prepositional cascade of Rom 11:36 (ek, dia, eis autou) closes the loop on the Pauline side. Analogia scripturae is invoked at the anthropomorphic complicating texts: the 'God smelled' of Gen 8:21 and Lev 1:9 is read against Ps 50:9-12 where God himself denies that sacrifices feed him; the 'God rested' of Gen 2:2 is read against Isa 40:28 where God 'does not faint or grow weary'. The grammatico-historical method with the accommodation principle handles both honestly. No non-default hermeneutic is required for the verdict; the canon's own self-interpretation is sufficient.

counter_witness.anchor: Athanasius, Contra Gentes and De Decretis (CCEL NPNF Series 2 Vol 4); Augustine, Confessions XIII and De Trinitate V; Aquinas, Summa Theologica I Q.3 A.4 'Whether essence and existence are the same in God' (CCEL)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Aquinas: in God essence is identical with existence, so God exists necessarily and a se. Athanasius and Augustine teach God alone has being from himself; every creature has being only as God grants it

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church paragraphs 212 to 213 on the divine name 'I AM WHO AM' (vatican.va)
counter_witness.key_phrase: CCC 213: 'The revelation of the ineffable name I AM WHO AM contains then the truth that God alone IS. God is the fullness of Being and of every perfection, without origin and without end.' Aseity explicitly tied to Exod 3:14

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession Article I, Of God (bookofconcord.org); Formula of Concord Solid Declaration
counter_witness.key_phrase: Augsburg I: 'One Divine Essence, eternal, without body, without parts, of infinite power, wisdom, and goodness, the Maker and Preserver of all things.' Eternity and Creator-status entail aseity in the Lutheran confessional grammar

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles, Article I, Of Faith in the Holy Trinity (churchofengland.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: 'There is but one living and true God, everlasting, without body, parts, or passions; of infinite power, wisdom, and goodness; the Maker and Preserver of all things.' Anglican confession of the self-existent eternal God

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith 2.2; Belgic Confession Article 1
counter_witness.key_phrase: WCF 2.2: 'God hath all life, glory, goodness, blessedness, in and of himself; and is alone in and unto himself all-sufficient.' Belgic 1 confesses one simple and spiritual Being, eternal, incomprehensible, almighty

counter_witness.anchor: United Methodist Articles of Religion, Article I, Of Faith in the Holy Trinity (umc.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: 'There is but one living and true God, everlasting, without body or parts, of infinite power, wisdom, and goodness; the maker and preserver of all things.' Methodist confession of the eternal self-existent God

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession of Faith 1632, Article 1, Of God and the Creation of All Things
counter_witness.key_phrase: 'We believe in one eternal, almighty, and incomprehensible God, the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost.' Eternity entails aseity; the Anabaptist tradition confesses divine self-existence with the historic church

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths, Truth 2, The One True God (ag.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: 'The one true God has revealed Himself as the eternally self-existent I AM, the Creator of heaven and earth and the Redeemer of mankind.' Pentecostal confession of aseity by direct reference to Exod 3:14

counter_witness.anchor: OCA, The Orthodox Faith, on God the Father (oca.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Orthodoxy teaches God as the uncreated, self-existent, eternal source of all being. 'God may certainly be said to exist perfectly and absolutely.' Aseity is presupposed in the Cappadocian doctrine of the Father as arche (source) of the Trinity

web.quote: Exod 3:14 ehyeh asher ehyeh, hayah H1961 qal imperfect 1cs doubled with relative pronoun. 'I AM WHO I AM'. Divine self-naming at the burning bush, ground of the Tetragrammaton
John 8:58 prin Abraam genesthai egō eimi. ginomai aorist middle infinitive of Abraham contrasted with eimi present indicative active 1s of Jesus. Direct echo of Exod 3:14 LXX
Acts 17:25 prosdeomai G4326 present middle participle negated: God is not served 'as one in need of additional thing'. Technical lexical statement of aseity by Paul on the Areopagus
Rom 11:36 ex autou kai di' autou kai eis auton ta panta. Triple prepositional cascade: God as source, instrument, and goal of all that is. No prior to whom he could be indebted
Ps 50:12 raab H7456 'if I were hungry'. Divine first-person denial that God needs to be fed: 'for the world and its fullness are mine'. Direct canonical statement of aseity
John 5:26 zōēn echein en heautō ('to have life in himself'). Technical NT expression of aseity, first of the Father, then granted to the Son. Ground of the doctrine of eternal generation
hayah H1961, the verb of being. Used in the qal imperfect 1cs at Exod 3:14 as the divine self-name. Foundational for the lexical claim of aseity
prosdeomai G4326, 'to need in addition, have further need'. Used at Acts 17:25 negated of God. The verb of additional need denied of the Creator
Gen 8:21 ruach H7306 hiphil 'smelled' plus reach H7381 nichoach H5207 'pleasing aroma'. Anthropomorphic complicating text; resolved by Ps 50:9-12 where God says he does not eat sacrifices
Gen 2:2 shabath H7673 'rested'. Anthropomorphic complicating text; resolved by Isa 40:28 where God 'does not faint or grow weary'. Pattern-setting, not recovery
Aquinas Summa I Q.3 A.4: 'In God essence and existence are the same.' Therefore God exists of himself necessarily. Locus classicus for aseity in the Thomistic tradition
CCC 213: 'The revelation of the ineffable name I AM WHO AM contains then the truth that God alone IS. God is the fullness of Being and of every perfection, without origin and without end'
Augsburg Confession Article I: 'One Divine Essence, eternal, without body, without parts, of infinite power, wisdom, and goodness, the Maker and Preserver of all things'
AG Statement of Fundamental Truths 2: 'The one true God has revealed Himself as the eternally self-existent I AM, the Creator of heaven and earth.' Direct reference to Exod 3:14
OCA: God is the uncreated and self-existent source of all being. Aseity is presupposed in the Cappadocian doctrine of the Father as arche (source) of the Trinity
